response: The EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), in force since May 2018, applies to any organization handling EU residents’ data and enshrines rights such as access, erasure, portability, and explicit consent, while obliging large-scale processors to appoint Data Protection Officers and report breaches within 72 hours. Violations carry fines of up to the greater of €20 million or 4 % of global annual revenue; headline penalties include €1.2 billion against Meta, €746 million against Amazon, and more than €200 million against Google. GDPR has become the template for laws like California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD, and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act, affirming data privacy as a fundamental right. Critics, however, warn of heavy compliance costs, uneven enforcement, and user fatigue from ubiquitous cookie-consent banners.
